question_text: A sample of ice at -5°C is heated steadily. It melts at 0°C and then the water is heated further until it boils at 100°C. During which process does the temperature remain constant even though heat energy is being added?
correct_answer: The temperature remains constant during melting (at 0°C) and during boiling (at 100°C). At these points, the heat energy being added goes into breaking the bonds between particles rather than increasing their kinetic energy (which would raise temperature).
explanation: During phase changes (melting and boiling), all the heat energy goes into changing the state of matter rather than increasing temperature. This is why the temperature plateaus on a heating curve.
